About: It is about the three Magi, represented as kings, who found Jesus by following a star and worshipped him.
It is about the gifts of gold, frankincense, and myrrh laid before Jesus.
It is about the biblical account of the Magi in the Gospel of Matthew.
It is about the expansion of the biblical account by later non-canonical sources like the Golden Legend.
It is about the idea that Jesus was recognized as king from his infancy.
It is about the representation of the Nativity and its importance in cycles of the Life of the Virgin and Life of Christ.
It is about stories speculating on the identity of the three kings.
It is about the representation of the three kings as symbolizing the three continents: Europe, Asia, and Africa.
It is about the prevailing names for the three kings: Gaspar, Melchior, and Balthasar.
It is about the theological significance, exotic attire, and expensive gifts associated with the story.
Affects: It affects the understanding of Jesus' recognition as king from infancy.
It affects the iconography of the Nativity scene.
It affects the depiction of the three Magi in art.
It affects the commemoration in Western Christianity as the Feast of the Epiphany.
It affects the commemoration in the Eastern Orthodox Church on the Feast of the Nativity.
AssociatedWith: It is associated with the Nativity of Jesus.
It is associated with the Bible, specifically Matthew 2:11.
It is associated with non-canonical sources like the Golden Legend.
It is associated with the theological concept of Jesus as king.

It is associated with the Feast of the Nativity.
It is associated with Christian iconography.
It is associated with the Vulgate Latin title 'A Magis adoratur'.

It is associated with Byzantine depictions of the Nativity.
